name: Romanée-Conti
description: The most prestigious vineyard in Burgundy, monopole of Domaine de la Romanée-Conti. Produces wines of extraordinary complexity and longevity.
historicalNotes: Named after the Prince de Conti who purchased it in 1760. Produces only 450 cases per year on average.

name: La Tâche
description: Monopole of DRC, larger than Romanée-Conti but equally prestigious. Known for power combined with elegance.
historicalNotes: Expanded to current size in 1930s when DRC acquired adjacent parcels.

name: Richebourg
description: Rich, powerful Grand Cru producing some of the most structured wines in Vosne-Romanée.
historicalNotes: Name means 'rich hillside.' Shared by multiple top producers including DRC, Leroy, Gros, and Méo-Camuzet.

name: Romanée-Saint-Vivant
description: Most delicate and feminine of Vosne's Grand Crus, known for elegance and aromatic complexity.
historicalNotes: Named after the Abbey of Saint-Vivant which owned the vineyard in medieval times. DRC owns large portion.

name: La Romanée
description: Tiny Grand Cru monopole of Comte Liger-Belair. Sits directly above Romanée-Conti.
historicalNotes: Smallest Grand Cru in Burgundy. Restored to greatness by Louis-Michel Liger-Belair in 2002.

name: La Grande Rue
description: Monopole of Domaine François Lamarche. Runs between La Tâche and Romanée-Conti.
historicalNotes: Promoted from Premier Cru to Grand Cru status in 1992.

name: Échezeaux
description: Largest Grand Cru in Vosne area with considerable variation between climats. Generally elegant style.
historicalNotes: Contains 11 different climats within its boundaries, leading to quality variation.

name: Grands-Échezeaux
description: More consistent and concentrated than Échezeaux, considered superior despite the name suggesting opposite.
historicalNotes: DRC holds significant parcels here producing benchmark examples.

name: Clos de Vougeot
description: Famous walled vineyard with 80+ owners. Quality varies significantly by parcel location and producer.
historicalNotes: Established by Cistercian monks in 1100s. The Renaissance château is headquarters of the Confrérie des Chevaliers du Tastevin.

name: Musigny
description: Epitome of elegance in red Burgundy. Small amount of Chardonnay also planted (Musigny Blanc).
historicalNotes: Dominated by Domaine Comte Georges de Vogüé who owns 70% of the vineyard.

name: Bonnes-Mares
description: Straddles Chambolle and Morey-Saint-Denis. More powerful and structured than typical Chambolle.
historicalNotes: Name possibly derives from 'bonnes mères' (good mothers) - the nuns who once tended the vines.

name: Clos de la Roche
description: Largest and most powerful of Morey's Grand Crus. Dense, age-worthy wines.
historicalNotes: Name refers to the rocky outcrop at top of vineyard.

name: Clos Saint-Denis
description: Village namesake. Elegant, refined wines with great aging potential.
historicalNotes: Named after the priory of Saint-Denis de Vergy. Village added its name in 1927.

name: Clos des Lambrays
description: Monopole promoted to Grand Cru in 1981. Combines power and elegance.
historicalNotes: Nearly destroyed by phylloxera, restored in 20th century. Now owned by LVMH.

name: Clos de Tart
description: Historic monopole producing rich, powerful wines with great longevity.
historicalNotes: Owned by Cistercian nuns for 800 years until French Revolution. Single-owner monopole since 1141.

name: Chambertin
description: Napoleon's favorite wine. Benchmark for powerful, structured Burgundy with massive aging potential.
historicalNotes: Village added '-Chambertin' to its name. Field of Bertin (Champ de Bertin) became Chambertin.

name: Chambertin-Clos de Bèze
description: Can be labeled as Chambertin. Equal in quality, sometimes more elegant.
historicalNotes: Older vineyard than Chambertin, planted by Abbey of Bèze monks in 7th century.

name: Chapelle-Chambertin
description: Lighter style than Chambertin but with classic Gevrey structure.
historicalNotes: Named after Notre-Dame de Bèze chapel that once stood here.

name: Charmes-Chambertin
description: Largest of Chambertin satellites. Charming, accessible style as name suggests.
historicalNotes: Includes Mazoyères-Chambertin which can also use Charmes name.

name: Griotte-Chambertin
description: Tiny Grand Cru producing delicate, perfumed wines. Name may refer to cherry (griotte) or limestone (griot).
historicalNotes: Smallest of the Chambertin satellites.

name: Latricières-Chambertin
description: Elegant, refined Grand Cru with excellent aging potential.
historicalNotes: Name derives from 'latricières' meaning poor, stony soil.

name: Mazis-Chambertin
description: Robust, structured wines closer in style to Chambertin than other satellites.
historicalNotes: Name possibly from 'mazis' meaning ruins or small houses.

name: Ruchottes-Chambertin
description: Highest elevation of Chambertin satellites. Elegant, mineral wines.
historicalNotes: Name from 'roches' (rocks) - rockiest of all Chambertins.

name: Corton
description: Largest Grand Cru in Burgundy. Only red Grand Cru in Côte de Beaune. Powerful, long-lived wines.
historicalNotes: Name from 'Cort d'Othon' (Othon's court) - Charlemagne's estate. Spans three villages.

name: Corton-Charlemagne
description: Majestic white Grand Cru with great power and longevity. Rivals top Montrachets.
historicalNotes: Legend says Charlemagne planted these slopes with white grapes after his wife complained red wine stained his beard.

name: Charlemagne
description: Tiny Grand Cru rarely used. Most producers prefer Corton-Charlemagne label.
historicalNotes: Wines can be labeled Corton-Charlemagne or just Charlemagne.

name: Montrachet
description: Most famous white wine vineyard in the world. Perfect balance of power, elegance, and complexity.
historicalNotes: Shared between Puligny and Chassagne. Both villages added Montrachet to their names. 'Mont Rachat' (bald mountain).

name: Bâtard-Montrachet
description: Rich, powerful white Grand Cru. More opulent than Montrachet.
historicalNotes: Legend says 'bastard' name comes from illegitimate child of lord of Montrachet. Shared by Puligny and Chassagne.

name: Bienvenues-Bâtard-Montrachet
description: Smallest Montrachet satellite entirely in Puligny. Elegant, refined style.
historicalNotes: Name means 'welcome to Bâtard-Montrachet.' Tiny production from handful of growers.

name: Chevalier-Montrachet
description: Most elegant Montrachet. Higher elevation, rockier soil produces tension and minerality.
historicalNotes: Sits directly above Montrachet. 'Knight' to Montrachet's 'King.'

name: Criots-Bâtard-Montrachet
description: Smallest Montrachet Grand Cru, entirely within Chassagne. Rich, powerful wines.
historicalNotes: Name from 'criots' meaning chalk in local dialect. Only a few producers.
